**Stakeholder-Based Design**  
QUESTIONS AND PARAPHRASED RESPONSES

1. In no particular order, what currently works well for you about the Canvas web app?  
   1. Student 1: Notifications are helpful, let you know when your work is graded  
   2. Student 2: File uploads, messages with classmates/professors  
   3. Student 3: Can turn in assignments and see assignment details  
2. What doesn’t work so well, or is a feature of Canvas you do not use in your day-to-day routine?  
   1. Student 1: Hard to organize group projects/submissions, all classes have differently set up pages  
   2. Student 2: Notifications are not helpful, lack upcoming assignment warning  
   3. Student 3: Never use messages, many professors do not organize their pages well which makes things harder to locate  
3. Outside of Canvas, what tools do you use to organize your school responsibilities?  
   1. Student 1: Physical notebook, google calendar, google doc  
   2. Student 2: Physical calendar, apple calendar, notes app  
   3. Student 3: Notes app, google calendar  
        
4. Do you make frequent use of Canvas’s calendar feature, or primarily use the application to turn in assignments?  
   1. Student 1: Both in approx. equal measure  
   2. Student 2: Only to turn in assignments  
   3. Student 3: Only to view and turn in assignments  
5. We are creating a calendar app that improves upon Canvas and Outlook by adding automation and seamless interactability. What features or problems would you like improved?  
   1. Student 1: For me, seeing multiple assignments on the same day  
   2. Student 2: Being able to sync my own events with canvas  
   3. Student 3: One problem I have is having to scroll far to find all my classes’ events

REFLECTIONS:

1. There was overlap in student responses, but the main notes were that grade-related notifications, communication \+ filesharing, and assignment related services were all appreciated. So, these are features we should not mess with in our application. If it isn’t broken, don’t fix it\!  
2. While we can’t do anything about how individual professors chose to set up their canvas pages (such as: files only being posted via announcements, or syllabus missing from “syllabus” page), we may be able to have our program search for all files in the class and group them by keyword or associated due/upload date. Also, we may want to see if we can do anything about the possibility of groupwork– “pulling” from groupmates’ calendars maybe? Simultaneous collaboration?  
3. Students seem to like the free-form concept of a notes app or physical journal for scheduling their work and deadlines. We may want to include such a feature in our design, maybe “sticky notes”?  
4. Canvas’s internal calendar seems to not get much use, it’s possible that students prefer using their dedicated calendar apps instead. Therefore, integrating their dedicated calendar apps into Canvas is probably a good idea.

**Functional Requirements**

There are no functional requirements for the app as-is; it is a browser-based calendar app. However, the integration with Canvas has a few functional requirements:

Functional Requirements for Accalendar: (What the system will or shall do)

- FR1: The system shall automatically synchronize data from canvas via the API consisting of upcoming assignments, deadlines, announcements, and events.  
  - User story:   
- FR2: The user will have the ability to manually create, edit, and delete personal events.  
  - User story: As a student with personal events recorded outside of canvas, I want to be able to manage those events so that I can curate the personal events that appear on canvas.  
- FR3: The system shall send out frequent alerts to the user to keep them updated, and posted on deadlines or new data being transferred from canvas.   
  - User story: As a busy student, I benefit from frequent reminder notifications, so I want alerts for upcoming deadlines that are synced to the latest Canvas information so that I do not miss any important deadlines or events.  
- FR4: The user will be able to easily view all assignments, labeled and organized events, and deadlines within the same calendar interface.   
  - User story: As a student with many responsibilities, I want to view my assignments, events, and deadlines altogether in one organized calendar interface so that I can read my schedule at a glance.  
- FR5: The [FullCalendar.js](http://FullCalendar.js) library that creates the calendar user interface shall have multiple views in the top right of the screen. It will allow the user to switch between daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly views. The purpose of this if the user wants to have a bigger view of everything on their calendar.  
  - User story: As a student planning both short-term and long-term tasks, I want to switch between daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly calendar views so that I can see my schedule at whatever level of detail I need at that moment.  
- FR6: Within the calendar cells in the system the user shall be able to drag and drop all events or items transferred or personally created. They can also stretch out an event vertically (based on the amount of time needed) and horizontally (based on the number of days needed).  
  - User story: As a student who prefers interacting with an interface (versus typing in adjustments), I want to drag, drop, and resize events on my calendar so that I can quickly adjust my schedule without reentering information.  
- FR7: The calendar interface will have arrow key buttons pointing left and right, like in a normal calendar application, to switch between years.   
  - User story: As an Accalender user, I want to be able to navigate my Canvas calendar like any other, and be able to switch between years using left and right arrows.  
- FR8: The system will organize and label data such as: deadlines, alerts and notifications, events, and assignments.   
  - User story:  As a student who wants to be organized, I want deadlines, notifications, events, and assignments to be clearly grouped and labeled so that I can quickly tell what each entry in my calendar represents.  
- FR9: The user will be greeted with a friendly UI welcoming and thanking them for using our application. Before redirecting them to the calendar interface the system will ask the user to register or login. In which the calendar will display all their data pulled from canvas after login.  
  - User story: As a user of the program, I want a protected login experience so that my calendar containing my personal and school events is protected.  
- FR10: The system will add a light or dark mode option. Some people prefer different visuals on their screens. A light and dark mode button would be a good addition.  
  - User story: As a user who prefers my apps to be in dark mode at night, I want an option to toggle between light and dark mode so that my calendar’s appearance is not jarring if I use it in the evening.

As a lightweight React app, there will be very little in the way of non-functional requirements, either. It will not be resource-intensive, and although it depends on a network connection to communicate with Canvas, manually scheduling events can be done offline.

Non-Functional Requirements for Accalendar: (How well the system carries out its functionalities)

- NFR1: After the user enters their personal information in the login UI, the system will take up to 3 seconds to validate their credentials and grant access to the applications features.  
  - User story: As a user of the app, I want my login to be verified quickly so that I can swiftly be redirected to my calendar.  
- NFR2: The system shall store user class data within a persistent database after querying the canvas API.  
  - User story: As a user of the app, I want my course and assignment information to be stored after I sync with canvas so that my information is easily accessible at every login.  
- NFR3: The calendar interface will begin to display updated user data within a minute or less after login or signup. This depends on how we implement the automatic data transfer.  
  - User story: As a user of the app, I want my data to sync quickly so that I don’t have to wait a long time to begin planning and organizing my schedule.  
- NFR4: User account credentials and API tokens will be hashed with SHA-256 and stored in a database.   
  - User story: As a user of the app,, I want my login credentials and API tokens to be securely protected in the system so that my personal and academic data is safe.  
- NFR5: The program will query the Canvas API for updates at a set interval (e.g. every 30 minutes by default, although this will be customizable) and apply these updates to the database.  
  - User story: As a user of the app, I want the system to regularly check Canvas for new or updated assignments while the program is open so that my calendar stays current without me having to refresh manually.

![][image1]  
![][image2]

**Press Release** 

Press release:

1. What it is  
   1. We are building a calendar application that will run in any web browser and allow you to sync your schedule with your assignments on Canvas.   
2. What it will do  
   1. All your assignments on Canvas will be seamlessly imported into your calendar. All you’ll have to do is log in and verify. Once you’re verified, the assignments will show up on the calendar automatically. From there you’ll be able to edit and remove them as needed. Once you submit the assignment on Canvas, that assignment will show up as completed on your calendar, too. Of course, you’ll also be able to add your own events and make the calendar work for you.  
3. Why you need it  
   1. Our app is built by students, for students. We know firsthand how busy your semester can get, and we’re building this app to smooth over some of the organizational snags that keep you from staying on top of your academics. With your personal calendars synched to Canvas, you’ll be able to see all your commitments in one place. That means that if you create an event in Google Calendar–say, a Zoom meeting with a professor–it’ll show up on your Canvas calendar as well with no extra inputting on your end.  
4. Future plans for this app  
   1. After the product is complete and deployed we intend to monitor its health, and fix any problems that may arise. As developers we need to ensure you, our user clients, continue to have a seamless experience interacting with our application. To do this we will be adding new features, and even improving on current features with each new version release. For example, integrating an AI model to enhance item navigation or to track even data, and a document scanner to capture physical calendars.

Feasibility:  
To our non-CS peers, the project seems feasible. Their main concern is integrating other applications such as google calendar, specifically whether automated synching between the calendars is doable. Their understanding is that the goal of the application is to make Canvas more of a “one-stop shop” for planning and scheduling all of their academic commitments, which is (in simple terms) what we intended. They understand that most of our planned capabilities have to do with revamping the calendar aspect of the application. However, after collecting student feedback, we are also interested in looking at improving other aspects of the application, such as organizing all files in a class regardless of which the professor has uploaded or attached them (syllabus, files, announcements, modules, etc.), as well as “Sticky Notes” for miscellaneous self-reminders. This possibility is interesting to our non-CS peers.

FAQ:

1. How can I get the application working in my Canvas?  
   1. Currently, it is a web application that requires you to verify in Okta to activate.  
2. If I use this application, will my professor’s side be different?  
   1. No, your professor will not see any difference on your student profile. Furthermore, your professor cannot see your synced calendars or events.  
3. Can I detach my personal calendars from Canvas if I decide I do not want them integrated?  
   1. Yes, you can detach a calendar at any time, or remove specific events that have synced from another calendar app onto Canvas if you do not wish for them to be present.
